query: best practices for Kubernetes pod disruption budgets

text_blocks.snippet: Setting up Pod Disruption Budgets (PDBs) is critical for keeping production applications highly available during cluster maintenance (like node upgrades and scale-downs). However, poor configuration can completely freeze cluster operations or cause unexpected downtime.
snippet_highlighted_words: keeping production applications highly available during cluster maintenance

text_blocks.snippet: Here are the industry best practices for configuring and managing PDBs:

The most common mistake is setting up a budget that leaves no room for disruptions. If your PDB leaves zero buffer, commands like kubectl drain will hang indefinitely, stalling cluster updates and autoscaling.

list.snippet:
Avoid matching total replicas: If a deployment has 3 replicas, setting minAvailable: 3 means 0 pods can be disrupted. Ensure that
minAvailable is strictly less than your replica count, or that maxUnavailable is at least 1.

Watch out for single-replica apps:
Never use minAvailable: 1 or minAvailable: 100% on a deployment with only 1 replica. This makes the node hosting it completely undrainable.

text_blocks.snippet: 2. Choose the Right Strategy for Your Workload Type
Match your PDB configuration style to how your specific application behaves:

list.snippet:
Use minAvailable for Quorum/Consensus Systems: For stateful apps like databases, etcd, or ZooKeeper, explicit counts matter
. Set minAvailable to match the exact number required to maintain a healthy quorum.

Use maxUnavailable: 1 as a Safe Default: For typical replicated stateless services, restricting eviction to one pod at a time is usually the safest approach to maintain performance while allowing upgrades to progress smoothly
.

Use Percentages for Dynamic/Autoscaling Apps: If a workload uses a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA), its replica count changes constantly. Hardcoded numbers will break as it scales. Instead, use percentage-based budgets (e.g., maxUnavailable: 25%) so the budget scales dynamically with the application
.

text_blocks.snippet: 3. Always Pair PDBs with Robust Health Checks
A PDB determines if a pod can be safely evicted by checking how many "healthy" pods are running.

list.snippet:
Implement Readiness Probes: If your pods lack readinessProbes, Kubernetes only looks at whether the container is running, not if it's actually ready to handle traffic
. A replacement pod might look "ready" to the PDB, but traffic will drop as soon as the old pod gets evicted.

Align with Deployment Update Strategies: Make sure your deployment's rollingUpdate strategy (maxSurge
and maxUnavailable) doesn't fight against your PDB constraints, which can stall routine rolling deployments.

text_blocks.snippet: A PDB protects your application from being torn down all at once, but it cannot prevent a single hardware node failure from taking out multiple pods.

list.snippet:
Combine your PDBs with podAntiAffinity or topologySpreadConstraints. Spreading your replicas across different nodes or availability zones ensures that when a single node is drained, only one replica is impacted, easily satisfying your PDB
.

text_blocks.snippet: 5. Set an Unhealthy Pod Eviction Policy
By default, if your application enters an unhealthy state (e.g., a bug causes pods to crash), a node drain will block indefinitely because the PDB is trying to wait for them to become healthy again.

list.snippet: For Kubernetes clusters running newer versions, utilize the unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: AlwaysAllow feature in your PDB spec. This ensures that if pods are already broken or misbehaving, they won't hold up node maintenance or cluster scaling.

text_blocks.snippet: Always ensure your PDB's matchLabels selector precisely mirrors the pod label selector used in your Deployment or StatefulSet. Accidental overlaps can result in one PDB locking down unrelated applications, while a selector mismatch will leave your intended app completely unprotected during an upgrade.
